1: Isaiah ben Amoz prophesied under four Judean kings: Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah (ca. 740-686 BCE), spanning the Assyrian crisis and its aftermath. His call-vision (Isaiah 6) was in the year Uzziah died — a political transition when the nation faced the rising Assyrian threat under Tiglath-Pileser III. Isaiah's ministry context: the Northern Kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BCE (referenced in Isa 7-8; 28); Jerusalem survived the siege of Sennacherib in 701 BCE (Isaiah 36-39). The 'Deutero-Isaiah' debate: many critical scholars argue that Isaiah 40-66 come from a later author writing during the Babylonian exile (ca. 550-530 BCE), given the specific naming of Cyrus (Isaiah 44:28; Isaiah 45:1) and the Babylonian setting of Isaiah 40-55. Conservative scholars hold single Isaianic authorship under divine foreknowledge. The canonical unity of the book is supported by the NT's consistent attribution of both halves to 'Isaiah.'
2: 'Hear, O heavens, and listen, O earth, for the LORD has spoken' — the opening of the covenant lawsuit (rib, legal dispute) genre. Calling heaven and earth as witnesses to the covenant violation follows the Deuteronomic pattern of Deuteronomy 4:26 ('I call heaven and earth as witnesses against you today') and Deuteronomy 30:19 ('I call heaven and earth to witness today that I have set before you life and death'). The covenant treaty was ratified before cosmic witnesses, and those same witnesses are summoned when one party violates it. The pattern is also in Micah 6:1-2 ('Hear, O mountains, the LORD's accusation') and Hosea 4:1.
3: 'An ox knows its owner and a donkey its master's feeding trough; but Israel does not know' — the irony is cutting: working animals demonstrate the basic loyalty to their provider that Israel withholds from YHWH. The ox and donkey were the standard team for agricultural labor in ancient Judah (Deuteronomy 22:10 prohibits yoking them together — the standard plowing pair). The image implies that the animals' instinctive recognition of provision ('who feeds me?') is more theologically perceptive than Israel's response to YHWH's centuries of covenant care. 'My people do not perceive' — the theme of Israel's cognitive failure (Isaiah will develop this into the hardening commission of Isaiah 6:9-10).
4: 'Woe to the sinful nation' (hoi goi chotel) — the first prophetic woe-oracle of Isaiah. The four-fold characterization: sinful nation, guilt-burdened people, evildoers' offspring, corrupting children. 'Holy One of Israel' (qedosh Yisrael) is Isaiah's signature epithet for YHWH, used approximately 26 times in Isaiah against 6 times in the rest of the OT. The holiness (qadosh) of YHWH is the dominant divine attribute in Isaiah (cf. the trisagion of Isaiah 6: 'Holy, holy, holy'). Israel's rebellion is named as 'turning their backs' — the covenant disloyalty visualized as turning away from the face-to-face relationship that characterized the Mosaic covenant.
5: 'Why do you invite more punishment? Why do you persist in rebellion?' — the rhetorical question of YHWH addressing Israel's self-destructive persistence. 'The whole head is diseased and the whole heart is failing' — the body-politic metaphor that will be developed in Isaiah 1:6-8: the entire sociopolitical organism of Judah is sick from head to foot. 'Heart is failing' (devai, sick/faint) suggests moral exhaustion and spiritual incapacity. The nation has no internal resources for self-correction — which is why external judgment is necessary to break the cycle.
6: 'From the sole of the foot to the top of the head there is not one healthy spot — only wounds, bruises, and open sores' — the medical diagnosis of the body politic. The head-to-foot formula for total bodily coverage is also used in Job 2:7 (Satan strikes Job with sores 'from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head'). The three injury types: petsaim (wounds from blows), chavurah (bruises/welts), and makka teriyyah (raw/festering sores). The sores are 'not cleansed or bandaged or soothed with oil' — no treatment has been applied. This is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:35 applied comprehensively.
7: 'Your land is desolate and your cities have been burned down; foreigners strip your countryside' — the historical setting for this oracle is likely Sennacherib's 701 BCE campaign, when 46 Judean cities were destroyed (according to the Taylor Prism annals) and thousands were deported. The Assyrian military practice of systematically destroying agricultural infrastructure (burning orchards, filling wells, destroying terraces) to depopulate an area is behind the 'desolate land' imagery. The Annals of Sennacherib describe Judah's countryside as stripped and Hezekiah 'like a bird in a cage' in Jerusalem.
8: 'The daughter of Zion is left like a temporary shelter in a vineyard, like a watchman's hut in a cucumber field' — the vivid similes of abandonment. The vineyard temporary shelter (sukkah) and cucumber-field hut were flimsy seasonal structures built during harvest for watchmen; they were abandoned after harvest and collapsed. Zion, once a great city, is reduced to a ramshackle structure in the middle of a stripped field. 'Like a city under siege' (kamivtsar) — the fortified city isolated and surrounded (the Assyrian siege being the immediate reference). The daughter-Zion (bat-Tsion) personification is used for Jerusalem as the communal daughter/city throughout the Prophets.
9: 'If the LORD of hosts had not spared us a tiny surviving remnant, we would have ended up like Sodom' — the remnant theology (she'ar, remnant/survival) that will become central to Isaiah's message (7:3 names his son Shear-jashub: 'a remnant will return'). YHWH's restraint alone prevented total destruction like Sodom and Gomorrah. Paul cites this verse (Romans 9:29) in his argument about Israel's remnant: even in judgment, YHWH preserves a seed. The comparison to Sodom/Gomorrah — the paradigmatic cities of total divine destruction (Gen 19) — sets the stakes of Judah's situation at the highest possible level.
10: 'Hear the word of the LORD, you rulers of Sodom! Listen to the teaching of our God, you people of Gomorrah!' — having just noted in Isaiah 1:9 that YHWH prevented Judah from becoming like Sodom, Isaiah immediately turns and addresses the leaders AS THOUGH they were the rulers of Sodom. The rhetorical shock is deliberate: you were spared from Sodom's fate but your conduct makes you Sodom's moral equivalent. The Sodom comparison was the most damning possible: Sodom's sin included social injustice (Ezekiel 16:49: 'She was arrogant, overfed, and unconcerned; she did not help the poor and needy') as well as the violence of Gen 19.
11: 'What do I care for the great number of your sacrifices? says the LORD' — the anti-sacrifice polemic: YHWH's rejection of Israel's ritual worship because it accompanies rather than replaces social justice. This is not a wholesale rejection of sacrifice (Isaiah later assumes the continuation of temple worship in the restored Zion); it is a rejection of sacrifice + wickedness as a combination. The same polemic is in Amos 5:21-24 ('I hate, I despise your religious festivals... but let justice roll on like a river'), Micah 6:6-8 ('What does the LORD require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly'), and Jeremiah 7:21-23 (the Temple Sermon).
12: 'When you come to stand before me, who asked you to trample through my courts?' — the divine sarcasm directed at ritual attendance without moral preparation. The verb 'trample' (ramas) applied to temple pilgrimage is deliberately offensive: YHWH describes the worshipers' presence in his sanctuary as trampling (as animals trample grain). The image inverts the honor of divine access — coming to the temple is a privilege, but without righteousness it becomes an imposition. The 'courts' (chatserot) of the temple were the outer spaces where the congregation gathered.
13: 'Stop bringing me worthless offerings; incense is detestable to me' — 'worthless offerings' (minchah shav) — the grain offering (minchah) that is shav (emptiness/vanity — the same word as 'vanity' in Ecclesiastes). Incense (qetoret), the sweet-smelling smoke that accompanied prayers in temple ritual (Psalms 141:2: 'May my prayer be set before you like incense'), is called 'detestable' (to'evah, abomination) — the strongest term of divine repugnance, normally reserved for Canaanite cult practices. 'New Moons, Sabbaths, and sacred assemblies' — the entire calendar of Israel's appointed worship (Lev 23) pronounced intolerable because accompanied by evil.
14: 'I utterly hate your new moon festivals and your appointed feasts; they have become a burden to me' — the divine burden-language: Israel's religious festivals have become oppressive weight (torach) on YHWH. The irony: the appointed feasts (mo'adim) were given precisely to celebrate YHWH's provision and covenant (Lev 23); now YHWH finds them tiresome. Jeremiah 6:20 develops the same theme: 'What do I care about incense from Sheba or sweet calamus from a distant land? Your burnt offerings are not acceptable; your sacrifices do not please me.' Ritual without relationship = burden, not blessing.
15: 'When you raise your hands in prayer, I will avert my eyes; your hands are soaked in blood' — hands raised in prayer (the orans posture of the ancient Near East, shown in Egyptian and Mesopotamian iconography) are the gesture of petition and praise. But the same hands are 'full of blood' (male damim) — soaked in violence, judicial murder, exploitation. The image connects worship posture to moral action: the body that kills in the marketplace cannot petition YHWH in the sanctuary. Psalms 24:4 requires 'clean hands and a pure heart' for approaching YHWH. The blood-soaked hands echo the Cain narrative (Genesis 4:10: 'your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground').
16: 'Wash and make yourselves clean; remove your evil deeds from my sight; stop doing evil' — the turn from indictment to imperative: after the lawsuit (Isaiah 1:2-15), YHWH now specifies the required response. The washing language ('wash yourselves,' hirchatshu) uses the verb for ritual washing before entering the sanctuary (Exodus 30:18-21; Leviticus 16:4), now applied to moral cleansing. The sequence: wash → be clean → remove evil acts → stop evil → learn good (Isaiah 1:16-17) is a compressed ethical curriculum. 'Remove your evil deeds from before my eyes' — YHWH's seeing/not-seeing is the theological reality: repentance makes evil deeds invisible to YHWH.
17: 'Learn to do what is right: pursue justice, correct the oppressor, defend the orphan, take up the widow's cause' — the classic prophetic justice quartet: justice (mishpat), the oppressor (chamots, literally 'the violent one'), the orphan (yatom), and the widow (almanah). The orphan-widow pair are the paradigmatic vulnerable in ancient Israelite society: without a male protector in a patrilineal society, they had no legal standing, no land inheritance, no economic security. Their cause is the specific locus of YHWH's justice concern (Deuteronomy 10:18: 'He defends the cause of the fatherless and the widow'; Deuteronomy 27:19: cursed is he who denies justice to the foreigner, orphan, or widow).
18: 'Come now, let us settle this between us, says the LORD: though your sins are like scarlet, they will be made white as snow' — the covenant lawsuit (rib) of Isaiah 1:2-17 reaches its resolution: YHWH offers negotiated settlement (nivachah, 'let us reason/argue/discuss the case'). The forensic legal setting gives way to a remarkable offer of full acquittal: scarlet (shani, the brilliant red dye from the scale insect Kermes, used in royal and tabernacle fabrics; Leviticus 14:4-6 uses scarlet thread in purification rites) made white as snow; crimson (tola'at, the worm that produces the dye) made as wool. The reversal from deep, ineradicable dye to pure white is the metaphor of divine forgiveness.
19: 'If you are willing and obedient, you will enjoy the best the land produces' — the two-way conditional that structures the Deuteronomic covenant: obedience produces land-blessing (Deuteronomy 28:1-14) and disobedience produces land-curse (Deuteronomy 28:15-68). 'Willing and obedient' (im-tovu ve'shema'tem, if you are willing and if you hear/obey) — the double condition linking inner disposition (willing, good) to outward compliance (hear/obey — shema is the covenant listening term of Deuteronomy 6:4). 'The best the land produces' (tov ha'arets, the goodness of the land) = the agricultural abundance of the covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 8:7-10).
20: 'But if you refuse and resist, you will be consumed by the sword — for the mouth of the LORD has spoken' — the covenant curse: refusal produces military destruction. 'Consumed by the sword' (cherev te'ukelu, the sword will eat you) — the sword as YHWH's instrument of covenant judgment is the language of Deuteronomy 28:25 and Leviticus 26:25. 'The mouth of the LORD has spoken' (ki pi YHWH dibber) is Isaiah's closing formula of divine authority, equivalent to the prophetic 'thus says the LORD.' It grounds the oracle in YHWH's own speech-act: what YHWH declares will come to pass because his word does not return empty (Isaiah 55:11).
21: 'How the once-faithful city has become a prostitute!' — the harlot-city metaphor (zonah, a female prostitute) applied to Jerusalem opens the communal lament/accusation that runs through Isaiah 1:21-26. The faithful city (qiryah ne'emanah) has become unfaithful (zonah). The prostitution metaphor for Israel's covenant disloyalty is developed most extensively in Hosea (Isaiah 1-3), Jeremiah (Isaiah 2:20; Isaiah 3:1-9), and Ezekiel (Isaiah 16; 23). The specific accusation here is civic/judicial corruption rather than literal sexual sin or religious harlotry. 'Once she was filled with justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedeq) made its home in her — but now it is murderers.'
22: 'Your silver has turned to worthless dross; your finest wine is diluted with water' — the debasement metaphors: silver that has been separated from its lead base (sug, dross/slag) is the waste product of smelting, not the refined metal. The diluted wine (sabekah bemayim, your wine cut with water) represents the fraudulent marketplace practices of Amos 8:5-6 (cheating with scales, selling the poor for a pair of sandals). Precious metals and fine wine were the currency of honor in the ancient world; their adulteration represents the moral debasement of Jerusalem's leadership class. The metallurgical imagery will return in Isaiah 1:25 (refining as divine purification).
23: 'Your rulers are rebels and partners in crime. Every one of them loves a bribe and chases after payoffs' — the comprehensive corruption of the leadership class. Three accusations: they are rebels (sorerim, stubborn/rebellious — the covenant violation term); they are partners with thieves (chaverei gannav); they love bribes (shochad) and chase payoffs (shalmoni, the returning of favors to patrons). The bribe-accepting judge was the paradigmatic corruption in Israelite society (Exodus 23:8: 'Do not accept a bribe, for a bribe blinds those who see and twists the words of the righteous'). 'They refuse to give the orphan a hearing, and the widow's case never reaches them' — the specific victims of bribe-corrupted justice are the vulnerable of Isaiah 1:17.
24: 'Therefore the Lord, the LORD of hosts, the Mighty One of Israel, declares: "Enough — I will relieve myself of my foes"' — the triple divine name (ha'adon, YHWH tseva'ot, avir Yisrael) is the most solemn invocation in the passage. The 'Mighty One of Israel' (avir Yisrael, literally 'Bull/Strong One of Israel') is an archaic divine epithet also in Genesis 49:24 ('because of the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel, the Mighty One of Jacob') and Psalms 132:2, 5. The image is of a warrior-king who has endured enough provocation and now declares action. 'I will relieve myself of my foes' — the Hebrew uses bodily language (anachemi, I will be comforted/relieved) suggesting divine emotional release through judgment.
25: 'I will raise my hand against you and refine away your dross as with lye and remove all your impurities' — the metallurgical refining metaphor: YHWH as the smelter who applies lye (bor, potassium carbonate used in ancient silver refining to remove lead and other impurities) to purify the corrupted metal. The raised hand (ashivah yadi alayich, I will return my hand against you) signals divine action and judgment (the same gesture as the Exodus plagues). But the goal is purification, not destruction: removing the dross and impurities (bedililayich, your alloy metals) to recover the pure silver. Judgment as purification rather than annihilation is the distinctive Isaianic note.
26: 'I will restore your judges as in former times and your counselors as in the early days. After that you will be called the City of Righteousness, the Faithful City' — the eschatological restoration oracle: YHWH will repair what the corruption of Isaiah 1:21-23 destroyed. 'Judges as in former times' (shoftayich keva'rishonah) and 'counselors as in the early days' — the model is the idealized Mosaic period or the early monarchy under David. The title the restored Jerusalem will bear: 'City of Righteousness' (ir hatsedek) and 'Faithful City' (qiryah ne'emanah) — the exact opposite of 'once-faithful city become prostitute' in Isaiah 1:21. The oracle moves from indictment to restoration through refining judgment.
27: 'Zion will be redeemed through justice (mishpat), and those among her who turn back to God, through righteousness (tsedaqah)' — the twin instruments of Zion's redemption: justice and righteousness, the same pair that characterized the ideal Jerusalem of Isaiah 1:21 ('justice made its home in her') and the corrupt Jerusalem of Isaiah 1:21-23 ('they refuse to give the orphan a hearing'). Redemption (tipadeh, will be redeemed — the root pdh, ransoming a slave or captive) will come through the social practices that should have prevented judgment. The penitents ('those who turn back') are the remnant (implied) who will populate the restored city.
28: 'But rebels and sinners will be crushed together, and those who abandon the LORD will be utterly destroyed' — the two-way division that closes the chapter: those who respond to YHWH's call (Isaiah 1:18-19) are redeemed; rebels/sinners/apostates are destroyed. 'Rebels' (poshe'im, covenant-breakers) and 'sinners' (chatta'im, moral offenders) and 'those who abandon the LORD' (ozeve Yhwh) — three ways of naming the covenant-disloyal. 'Utterly destroyed' (yiklu, they will be consumed/finished) is the language of total consumption, parallel to the fire of Isaiah 1:31.
29: 'For you will be put to shame over the sacred oak trees you desired, and humiliated over the ritual gardens you chose' — the Canaanite cult practices: 'sacred oak trees' (elim, terebinths/oaks — the ha'elim, the strong trees under which Baal and Asherah were worshiped at the high places) and 'ritual gardens' (ganot, gardens for fertility cult worship). The practice of planting sacred gardens for Asherah worship and conducting fertility rites under large trees is attested throughout the prophets (Hosea 4:13: 'They sacrifice on the mountaintops and burn offerings on the hills, under oak, poplar, and terebinth, because their shade is good'). 'Shame' and 'humiliation' — the honor-shame reversal for the idol-worshipers.
30: 'You will be like an oak tree with withering leaves, and like a garden with no water' — the fitting punishment matches the crime: those who worshiped under the sacred oaks become like a withering oak; those who cultivated ritual gardens become like a garden without water. The curse of Deuteronomy 28:23-24 (bronze sky, powdery earth, no rain) is applied specifically to the practices the worshipers chose. The desiccated garden is the antithesis of Psalms 1:3 ('a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in season and whose leaf does not wither') — the wicked become the anti-tree rather than the blessed tree.
31: 'The powerful will become like tinder, and their deeds like a spark; both will burn together and no one will put out the fire' — the closing fire image. 'The powerful' (hechazon, the strong one) become combustible material (ne'orts, tinder/hemp-like fiber); their deeds (po'olo, his work) become the spark (nitstots) that ignites it. The combination of the powerful person and their evil deeds produces unstoppable self-consuming fire — divine judgment that no human intervention can extinguish (contrast the intercession of Amos 7:2-5 where Amos successfully pleads for YHWH to relent; here no such intercession is possible). The chapter that opened with covenant lawsuit closes with unquenchable fire.
